Idiom (Attié): Koklo tshɔ gbɛgblɛ ’mi, yɛ li sɔ fɛ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed proverbial clause with concessive animal-subject imagery; SVO main clause with negated evaluative predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: An enemy’s friendly gesture may hide fear, pressure, or calculation rather than goodwill.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sran nɛ yɛ kpa nɔ, yɛ la yɛ yí kpa ɔn.
Usage Contexts: politics, conflict mediation, village warnings, sarcasm, workplace alliances
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Motif aligned with an Attié proverb reported in Ivorian proverb scholarship: the rooster’s dance before the hawk is not pleasure; tied to forest-village observations of predator and prey.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [kòkló t͡ʃɔ ɡ͡bɛ̀ɡ͡blɛ́ ʔmí | jɛ̀ lì sɔ̀ fɛ́]
First Example Sentence: Achi nɔ sɔ Seka, mɛ n bɔ yɛ: Koklo tshɔ gbɛgblɛ ’mi, yɛ li sɔ fɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Nɛ yɔ bɔ kɛ nɔ, na wɔn li koklo tshɔ gbɛgblɛ ’mi nɔ.
Third Example Sentence: ˮVin nɔ bɔ dzhë fɛ, mɛ wɔn yí nɔ ka: Koklo tshɔ gbɛgblɛ ’mi, yɛ li sɔ fɛ.

Idiom (Attié): Kpakpa lɔɔ nɔ, a ma yɔ tsɛ nɛ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative imperative proverb; fixed clause with addressee subject and impossible-object complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do not demand from someone what they are visibly unable to provide.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A ma yɔ sran nɛ yɛ ma nɔ ˗ka ɔn.
Usage Contexts: family requests, resource negotiation, leadership, market credit, practical advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Motif aligned with a published Attié proverb about not asking a crouched toad for a stool; reflects everyday reasoning about visible limits.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [k͡pák͡pá lɔ̀ɔ̀ nɔ̀ | à mà jɔ̀ t͡sɛ̀ nɛ́]
First Example Sentence: Yapi nɔ ma wɔli ɔn, mɛ n ka yɛ: Kpakpa lɔɔ nɔ, a ma yɔ tsɛ nɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Ɛ yɛ ma gbɛ nɛ, ɛn n bɔ: Kpakpa lɔɔ nɔ, a ma yɔ tsɛ nɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Nɔ nɔ li ˗ka fɛ, a ma yɔ tsɛ nɛ kpakpa lɔɔ nɔ.

Idiom (Attié): Bɔnɔ wɛ yɛ jɛ, mɛ bɔ a nɔ fɛ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated proverbial clause; topicalized body-part noun phrase followed by contrastive clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Family ties may be distant or complicated, but harm to one member is felt by the whole kin network.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mɔɔn nɔ yɛ dzhë fɛ, abusua nɔ yɛ wɔn nɔ.
Usage Contexts: family council, inheritance disputes, kinship, reconciliation, elder speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Motif aligned with a published Attié proverb about the monkey’s long tail feeling the pinch; used to frame the complexity of kinship bonds.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [bɔ̀nɔ́ wɛ́ jɛ́ | mɛ̀ bɔ̀ à nɔ̀ fɛ́]
First Example Sentence: Abe nɔ sɔ abusua nɔ, mɛ wɔn bɔ: Bɔnɔ wɛ yɛ jɛ, mɛ bɔ a nɔ fɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Nɛ ba nɔ yɛ dzhë ɔn, bɔnɔ wɛ yɛ jɛ mɛ bɔ a nɔ fɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Nana nɔ ka nɛ, bɔnɔ wɛ yɛ jɛ, mɛ bɔ a nɔ fɛ, ɛn yɔ kpa.

Idiom (Attié): A si mɛn ɛmɔ nɔ, a li yɛ dzhë ɔn.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional clause with second-person subject; epistemic conclusion in the main clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: You must know a person closely before judging their flaws or hidden burden.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A si sran nɔ kpa, ɛn a yɔ bɔ yɛ nɔ.
Usage Contexts: friendship, marriage advice, conflict resolution, character assessment, teaching
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Motif aligned with a published Attié proverb about approaching the fromager tree before seeing its cracks; rooted in forest ecology and social caution.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [à sì mɛ́n ɛ̀mɔ́ nɔ̀ | à lì jɛ̀ d͡ʒɛ̀ ɔ̃]
First Example Sentence: Nɛ yɛ sran nɔ bɔ fɛɛ, a si mɛn ɛmɔ nɔ, a li yɛ dzhë ɔn.
Second Example Sentence: N’cho nɔ bɔ yɛ kɛ, mɛ n ka: A si mɛn ɛmɔ nɔ, a li yɛ dzhë ɔn.
Third Example Sentence: Seka yɔ bɔ wɔn nɔ ɔn, ɛn n yɛ dzhë: a si mɛn ɛmɔ nɔ, a li yɛ dzhë ɔn.

Idiom (Attié): ’Wan yɛ gbɔɔn, be˗ yɛ yɛn nɔ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed biclausal metaphor; instrument noun as subject followed by understood companion instrument in a response clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Insiders understand a warning or tension before outsiders notice it.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sran nɛ wɔli nɔ li ˗ka nɔ sɔɔn.
Usage Contexts: family signals, workplace subtext, coded speech, ceremonial settings, gossip control
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Source-informed synthetic candidate using mortar-and-pestle imagery central to food preparation; requires native-speaker validation for idiomatic status.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [ʔwán jɛ̀ ɡ͡bɔ̀ɔ̀n | bè jɛ̀ jɛ́n nɔ̀]
First Example Sentence: Mɛ nɛ yí nɔ fɔ, ’wan yɛ gbɔɔn, be˗ yɛ yɛn nɔ.
Second Example Sentence: Adzopé nɔ, nana nɔ bɔ fɛɛ; ’wan yɛ gbɔɔn, be˗ yɛ yɛn nɔ.
Third Example Sentence: A ma sɔ ˗ka nɔ kpɛ, ’wan yɛ gbɔɔn ɔn, be˗ yɛ yɛn nɔ.

Idiom (Attié): Mɔɔn tsɛ nɛ, bɔsɔɓo yɛ shë.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Temporal proverbial clause with inanimate subject; personified road as second-clause subject.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When trouble, opportunity, or news begins, the path of action quickly becomes active.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: ˗Ka nɔ ba nɔ, sran nɔ bɛ yɔ dzhë fɛ.
Usage Contexts: travel, market news, emergency planning, community mobilization, youth advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Source-informed synthetic candidate using river-and-road imagery from the Akyé forest zone between major waterways; requires native-speaker validation for idiomatic status.
First Example Sentence: Mɔɔn tsɛ nɛ, bɔsɔɓo yɛ shë; ɛn yɛ kɔ Alépé fɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Nɛ sɛn nɔ ba, mɔɔn tsɛ nɛ bɔsɔɓo yɛ shë.
Third Example Sentence: A bɔ wɔn nɔ kpa ɔn, mɔɔn tsɛ nɛ, bɔsɔɓo yɛ shë.

Idiom (Attié): Gbɛ nɔ sɛn ɔn, sɛn yɛ ma kɔ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Agricultural proverb with delayed-result structure; topical noun plus dependent return clause.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Results come after patience, preparation, and the right season, not from impatience.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A dyi nɔ kpa, ɛn fɛ nɔ ba.
Usage Contexts: farming, schooling, apprenticeships, parenting, business patience
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Source-informed synthetic candidate grounded in Akyé agricultural life and seasonal rain cycles; requires native-speaker validation for idiomatic status.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [ɡ͡bɛ̀ nɔ̀ sɛ́n ɔ̃ | sɛ́n jɛ̀ mà kɔ̀]
First Example Sentence: Yɛ kpa nɔ ba fɛɛ, gbɛ nɔ sɛn ɔn, sɛn yɛ ma kɔ.
Second Example Sentence: ˮVin nɔ dyi lɔ nɔ, n bɔ yɛ: Gbɛ nɔ sɛn ɔn, sɛn yɛ ma kɔ.
Third Example Sentence: Nɛ ɛn yɔ kpa nɛ, gbɛ nɔ sɛn ɔn, sɛn yɛ ma kɔ.

Idiom (Attié): ’Mi nɔ li ˗ka, yí yɛ kɔ gbɛ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metaphoric contrast clause; body-part subject contrasted with inner-state subject and directional verb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone may speak calmly while carrying resentment, worry, or unresolved intent inside.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sran nɔ bɔ kpa, mɛ yɛ yí nɔ li ɔn.
Usage Contexts: conflict mediation, marriage, apology, leadership, psychological nuance
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Source-informed synthetic candidate drawing on mouth/heart contrast found widely in Akan-area moral speech; requires native-speaker validation for idiomatic status.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [ʔmí nɔ̀ lì kà | jí jɛ̀ kɔ̀ ɡ͡bɛ̀]
First Example Sentence: A bɔ kpa fɛɛ, mɛ ’mi nɔ li ˗ka, yí yɛ kɔ gbɛ.
Second Example Sentence: Nana nɔ yɛn ɛn: ’Mi nɔ li ˗ka, yí yɛ kɔ gbɛ.
Third Example Sentence: Nɛ yɛ yɔ bɔ lɔ nɔ, ’mi nɔ li ˗ka, yí yɛ kɔ gbɛ.

Idiom (Attié): Melu kɔ dzhë, mɔn yɛ si ɔn.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Biclausal proverb with motion verb followed by locative-memory predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person may leave home or hide a deed, but origins and traces remain known.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sran nɔ kɔ, mɛ yɛ bɔsɔ nɔ yɛn yɛ.
Usage Contexts: migration, accountability, ancestry, village memory, public reputation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Source-informed synthetic candidate using boat-and-water imagery consistent with Akyé riverine geography and migration narratives; requires native-speaker validation for idiomatic status.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [mèlú kɔ̀ d͡ʒɛ̀ | mɔ̃̀n jɛ̀ sì ɔ̃]
First Example Sentence: Melu kɔ dzhë, mɔn yɛ si ɔn; a ma mɛn abusua nɔ.
Second Example Sentence: Yapi kɔ Abidjan, mɛ melu kɔ dzhë, mɔn yɛ si ɔn.
Third Example Sentence: Nɛ yɛ la ˗ka nɔ, melu kɔ dzhë, mɔn yɛ si ɔn.

Idiom (Attié): Sran nɛ gbɔɔn ɔn, dzhigbo nɔ ma nɔ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative-clause proverb with social-group object; nominalized actor plus obligation predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A loud speaker or public boaster becomes accountable to the community they address.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Sran nɛ bɔ lɔ nɔ, ɛn yɛ yɔ ˗ka nɔ kpa.
Usage Contexts: politics, youth associations, age-grade leadership, public promises, satire
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Source-informed synthetic candidate referencing Akyé age-class organization and public responsibility; requires native-speaker validation for idiomatic status.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Attié): [sɾán nɛ̀ ɡ͡bɔ̀ɔ̀n ɔ̃ | d͡ʒìɡ͡bó nɔ̀ mà nɔ̀]
First Example Sentence: Atsé nɔ bɔ gbɔɔn, sran nɛ gbɔɔn ɔn, dzhigbo nɔ ma nɔ.
Second Example Sentence: Nɛ chef nɔ ka fɛɛ, sran nɛ gbɔɔn ɔn, dzhigbo nɔ ma nɔ.
Third Example Sentence: Yɛ ma yɛ fɛ ɔn, mɛ sran nɛ gbɔɔn ɔn, dzhigbo nɔ ma nɔ.
